title: Developers Seek Financial Reforms to Unlock Housing Investment
description: Developers Seek Financial Reforms to Unlock Housing Investment  nigeriahousingmarket.com
content: Lack of Funding Continues to Stall Real Estate Development in Nigeria Housingnational news Jun 18 Written By Ayomide Fiyinfunoluwa Funding Constraints Slow Growth in Nigeria’s Real Estate Sector Limited access to financing remains one of the biggest obstacles facing Nigeria’s real estate sector, with developers warning that inadequate funding is slowing project delivery, increasing housing costs and worsening the country's housing deficit. Industry stakeholders say the challenge has become more pronounced amid rising construction costs, high interest rates and restricted access to long-term capital. The funding gap is affecting developers of all sizes, from affordable housing providers to large-scale estate projects, limiting the sector's ability to meet growing housing demand across the country. / You Might Also Like / The 2026 Dangote Refinery Blueprint: Location, IPO, and Global Energy Impact The Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway: Transforming Nigeria’s Infrastructure Landscape Nigeria’s Housing Deficit: How Big Is the Problem in 2026? Mistakes First-Time Property Investors Make in Nigeria Developers Face Persistent Financing Constraints Real estate experts note that access to affordable, long-term financing remains a critical challenge for housing development in Nigeria. Many developers rely heavily on commercial bank loans, which often carry high interest rates and relatively short repayment periods. These financing structures are frequently unsuitable for real estate projects that require significant capital investment and extended development timelines. Stakeholders argue that the mismatch between project financing needs and available funding options continues to slow the pace of housing delivery. Rising Costs Compound Development Challenges The financing challenge comes at a time when developers are also grappling with rising construction costs. Inflation, foreign exchange volatility and higher prices for building materials have significantly increased project expenses. As a result, many developers are finding it difficult to secure sufficient capital to complete ongoing projects or launch new developments. Industry participants warn that these pressures are contributing to higher property prices and reducing housing affordability for many Nigerians. Impact on Housing Supply The shortage of development finance has direct implications for housing supply and the broader property market. Nigeria continues to face a significant housing deficit, with demand for quality and affordable housing far exceeding available supply. Developers say inadequate financing limits the number of projects that can be delivered annually, making it more difficult to close the housing gap. Experts note that increasing housing production will require stronger collaboration between government, financial institutions and private-sector developers. Need for Mortgage and Housing Finance Reforms Stakeholders are calling for reforms that expand access to long-term housing fi
summary: The lack of funding in Nigeria's real estate sector poses a significant challenge to developers, including Mixta Africa, as it slows project delivery, increases housing costs, and worsens the country's housing deficit, ultimately affecting the Lagos real estate market.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This affects our strategic priority of recovering concentrated receivables, as delayed project delivery and increased costs may lead to further receivable concentration.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: This may impact our pricing strategy, as developers may pass on increased costs to buyers, affecting our competitive positioning.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: We can leverage our relationships with institutional investors and bulk transaction partners to access long-term capital and mitigate the impact of funding constraints.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The risk of delayed project delivery and increased costs threatens our execution and ability to meet our revenue targets.

summary: The passage of the bipartisan housing bill in the US is expected to have a positive impact on the global real estate market, particularly in emerging markets like Lagos, where demand for affordable housing is high. This could lead to increased investment and development in the sector, benefiting Mixta Africa's strategic priorities. The bill's focus on affordable housing may also shift the market mix towards higher-margin segments, aligning with Mixta Africa's goals.

title: Nigerian banking elites shift wealth to London property market
description: Nigerian banking elites shift wealth to London property market  Business News Nigeria
content: Source: Bloomberg Share Nigeria’s corporate elite and leading bankers are quietly accelerating their investments in London real estate, underscoring a widening disconnect between the country’s harsh economic realities and the offshore wealth strategies of its richest citizens. Over the past year alone, a string of high-profile purchases has brought renewed scrutiny to Nigerian capital flowing into the UK property market. Notable disclosures include reports linking the late Herbert Wigwe, former Group CEO of Access Bank, to over 100 London properties through offshor Nigeria’s corporate elite and leading bankers are quietly accelerating their investments in London real estate, underscoring a widening disconnect between the country’s harsh economic realities and the offshore wealth strategies of its richest citizens. title: Nigeria banking tycoon Jim Ovia turns to luxury housing, calls property a better bet than banking
description: Nigeria banking tycoon Jim Ovia turns to luxury housing, calls property a better bet than banking  Business Insider Africa
content: Jim Ovia, the founder of Zenith Bank, is expanding his footprint in Nigeria's luxury real estate market, betting that high-end property offers stronger returns than traditional banking investments. Tycoon who built Nigeria’s biggest bank by market value says real estate beats banking Jim Ovia, founder of Zenith Bank, is investing heavily in Nigeria's luxury real estate market, seeing it as more profitable than traditional banking. His company, Quantum Luxury Properties Ltd, is developing two major high-end residential towers in Lagos, with units starting at $1.85 million and $2.8 million. Lagos is experiencing increased demand for premium properties, fueled by wealthy locals and Nigerians abroad taking advantage of currency fluctuations. Analysts expect the luxury real estate market in Lagos to grow, with over 1,400 new high-end homes projected by 2029. ADVERTISEMENT Recommended articles markets Nigeria’s 2027 payment data deadline puts banks, fintechs and cloud providers on notice markets Nigeria's telecom regulator under pressure to justify rules on $295 million airtime credit market markets Nigeria calls on researchers and business folks to investigate why its food is being rejected by Taboolaby Taboola Sponsored LinksSponsored Links Promoted LinksPromoted Links You May Like Doctors Identify 10 Medications Now Connected to Memory Decline (See the List)Neuro Research Center Learn More Everyone Laughed at Him When He Married Her, 6 Years Later She Shows Her MetamorphosisWatch what happenedTips and Tricks Jim Ovia, the founder of Zenith Bank, is expanding his footprint in Nigeria's luxury real estate market, betting that high-end property offers stronger returns than traditional banking investments. The 74-year-old billionaire, who built Zenith Bank from a $4 million startup in 1990 into Nigeria's largest lender by market value, is developing two luxury residential towers in Lagos through his company, Quantum Luxury Properties Ltd, according to Bloomberg. The first project, Metropolitan Towers, is a 26-floor residential development where units start at $1.85 million. Ovia is also nearing completion of Quantum Luxury Towers, a 44-unit high-rise with apartments priced from $2.8 million. ADVERTISEMENT The developments come as Lagos experiences growing demand for premium residential properties, driven by wealthy local investors seeking to preserve wealth and Nigerians in the diaspora taking advantage of a weaker naira to acquire assets at home. Tycoon who built Nigeria’s biggest bank by market value says real estate beats banking BI Africa According to property intelligence firm Estate Intel, about 135 homes in Lagos are currently priced above $1 million. The market is expected to expand significantly over the next few years, with nearly 1,400 additional luxury homes projected to enter the market by 2029. Developers have increasingly focused on high-end residential projects, particularly in affluent districts such as Victoria Island, Ikoyi, and Eko A
summary: Jim Ovia's entry into luxury real estate in Lagos signals a growing demand for premium properties, which may drive up prices and attract more high-end developers, ultimately benefiting Mixta Africa's premium Lakowe Lakes products and LNT commercial plots.
